data.title: Why is everything except cryptocurrencies rising?
data.description:
Author: EeeVee

"As long as you don't invest in crypto, you can make money on everything else."

Recently, the cryptocurrency market and other global markets seem to be experiencing starkly contrasting fortunes.

Throughout 2025, gold rose by over 60%, silver surged by 210.9%, and the US Russell 2000 index rose by 12.8%. Bitcoin, however, after a brief period of new highs, closed the year with a negative candle.

The divergence is intensifying at the start of 2026. On January 20th, gold and silver both reached new highs, the US Russell 2000 index outperformed the S&P 500 for 11 consecutive days, and the A-share STAR Market 50 index rose by over 15% in a single month.

In contrast, Bitcoin experienced its sixth consecutive day of decline on January 21st, plummeting from $98,000 back below $90,000 without looking back. After the October 11th crash, funds seemed to have decisively left the cryptocurrency market. BTC has been fluctuating below $100,000 for over three months, and the market has entered a period of "the lowest volatility in history." Disappointment is spreading among cryptocurrency investors. When asked about their success in making money in other markets after leaving Crypto, some even share the "ABC" secret: "Anything But Crypto"—as long as they don't invest in Crypto, they can make money in everything else. The "Mass Adoption" everyone was anticipating in the last round has indeed arrived. However, instead of the widespread adoption of decentralized applications that everyone expected, it's a complete "assetization" driven by Wall Street. This round, the US establishment and Wall Street are embracing Crypto with unprecedented enthusiasm. The SEC approved spot ETFs; BlackRock and JPMorgan Chase have allocated assets to Ethereum; the US has included Bitcoin in its national strategic reserves; pension funds in several states have invested in Bitcoin; and even the NYSE has announced plans to launch a cryptocurrency trading platform. So here's the question: Why, after receiving so much political and capital backing, has Bitcoin's price performance been so disappointing while precious metals and the stock market are hitting new highs? And why isn't Bitcoin following suit when cryptocurrency investors routinely use pre-market US stock prices to predict cryptocurrency trends? Why is Bitcoin so weak? Leading Indicator: Bitcoin is a "leading indicator" of global risk assets. Raoul Pal, founder of Real Vision, has repeatedly mentioned this in many of his articles. Because Bitcoin's price is purely driven by global liquidity and is not directly affected by any country's financial reports or interest rates, its volatility often precedes that of mainstream risk assets like the Nasdaq index. According to MacroMicro data, Bitcoin's price turning points have repeatedly preceded the S&P 500 index in recent years. Therefore, once Bitcoin's upward momentum, as a leading indicator, stalls and fails to reach new highs, it constitutes a strong warning signal that the upward momentum of other assets may also be nearing exhaustion. Secondly, Bitcoin's price, to this day, remains highly correlated with the net liquidity of the global US dollar. Although the Federal Reserve cut interest rates in 2024 and 2025, the quantitative tightening (QT) that began in 2022 continues to withdraw liquidity from the market. Bitcoin's record high in 2025 was largely driven by new funds brought in by ETFs, but this did not fundamentally change the tight global macro liquidity situation. Bitcoin's sideways movement is a direct reflection of this macro reality. In an environment of scarce liquidity, it's difficult for it to launch a super bull market. Meanwhile, the world's second-largest source of liquidity—the Japanese yen—is also tightening. The Bank of Japan raised its short-term policy rate to 0.75% in December 2025, the highest level in nearly 30 years. This directly impacts a crucial source of funding for global risk assets over the past few decades: yen carry trades. Historical data shows that since 2024, each of the Bank of Japan's three rate hikes has been accompanied by a Bitcoin price drop of over 20%. The synchronized tightening by the Federal Reserve and the Bank of Japan has further exacerbated the global liquidity environment.

The drop in cryptocurrency prices with each interest rate hike in Japan

Geopolitical conflicts

Finally, the potential "black swan" events in geopolitics are keeping market nerves on edge, and Trump's series of domestic and international actions in early 2026 have pushed this uncertainty to a new level.

Internationally, the Trump administration's actions are highly unpredictable.

From military intervention in Venezuela and the arrest of its president (unprecedented in the history of modern international relations), to the looming threat of war with Iran again; from attempting to forcibly purchase Greenland to issuing new tariff threats against the European Union, this series of radical unilateralist actions is comprehensively escalating tensions between major powers. Domestically in the United States, his actions have further fueled deep anxieties about a constitutional crisis. He not only proposed renaming the "Department of Defense" to the "War Department," but has also ordered active-duty troops to prepare for potential domestic deployments. These actions, combined with his previous remarks hinting at regret for not using military intervention and his reluctance to lose the midterm elections, have made public concerns increasingly clear: Will he refuse to accept defeat in the midterm elections and use force to secure re-election? This speculation and pressure are already exacerbating internal contradictions in the United States, and there are signs that protests across the country are expanding.

Last week, Trump invoked the Insurrection Act and deployed troops to Minnesota to quell protests. The Pentagon has since ordered approximately 1,500 active-duty soldiers stationed in Alaska to stand by.

The normalization of such conflicts is dragging the world into a "gray area" between localized wars and a new Cold War. Traditional full-scale hot wars have relatively clear paths, market expectations, and have even been accompanied by monetary easing to "rescue" the market.

However, these localized conflicts are highly uncertain, filled with "unknown unknowns."

For risk capital markets that heavily rely on stable expectations, such uncertainty is fatal. When large capital cannot predict future trends, the most rational choice is to increase cash holdings and remain on the sidelines, rather than allocating funds to high-risk, high-volatility assets. Why aren't other assets falling? In stark contrast to the stagnation of the cryptocurrency market, since 2025, precious metals, US stocks, and A-shares have all seen successive rises. However, these market rises are not due to a general improvement in macroeconomic and liquidity fundamentals, but rather a structural market driven by sovereign will and industrial policies against the backdrop of great power rivalry. The rise in gold prices is a reaction of sovereign states to the existing international order, rooted in the cracks in the credibility of the dollar system. The 2008 global financial crisis and the 2022 freeze on Russia's foreign exchange reserves completely shattered the myth of the "risk-free" nature of the dollar and US Treasury bonds as the ultimate global reserve assets. Against this backdrop, global central banks have become "price-insensitive buyers." They buy gold not for short-term profits, but to find an ultimate store of value that does not rely on the credit of any single sovereign power. Data from the World Gold Council shows that in 2022 and 2023, global central banks' net gold purchases exceeded 1,000 tons for two consecutive years, setting a historical record. This round of gold price increases was primarily driven by official forces, rather than market-driven speculative forces. The rise in the stock market reflects national industrial policies. Whether it's the US's "AI nationalization" strategy or China's "industrial self-reliance" policy, both involve deep state intervention and control of capital flows. Taking the US as an example, the "Chip and Science Act" has elevated the artificial intelligence industry to a strategic level of national security. Funds are clearly flowing out of large-cap tech stocks and into smaller, more growth-oriented stocks that align with policy direction. In China's A-share market, funds are also highly concentrated in sectors closely related to national security and industrial upgrading, such as "information technology innovation" and "defense and military industry." This government-driven market trend has a fundamentally different pricing logic from Bitcoin, which relies on purely market-based liquidity. Will history repeat itself? Historically, this isn't the first time Bitcoin has diverged from other assets. And each divergence has ultimately ended with a strong Bitcoin rebound. Historically, Bitcoin's RSI (Relative Strength Index) relative to gold has fallen below 30 four times, indicating extreme oversold conditions: 2015, 2018, 2022, and 2025. Each time, when Bitcoin was extremely undervalued relative to gold, it foreshadowed a rebound in the exchange rate or Bitcoin price.

Historical Bitcoin/Gold Price Movements, with RSI Indicator Below

In 2015, at the end of the bear market, Bitcoin's RSI relative to gold fell below 30, subsequently initiating the super bull market of 2016-2017.

In 2018, during the bear market, Bitcoin fell by more than 40%, while gold rose by nearly 6%. After the RSI fell below 30, Bitcoin rebounded by more than 770% from its 2020 low.

In 2022, during the bear market, Bitcoin fell by nearly 60%. After its RSI fell below 30, Bitcoin rebounded, outperforming gold again. From the end of 2025 to now, we have witnessed this historic oversold signal for the fourth time. Gold surged 64% in 2025, while Bitcoin's RSI relative to gold fell into oversold territory again. Is it still worthwhile to chase other assets now? Amid the clamor of "ABC" (Alibaba, Tencent, ABC), easily selling crypto assets to chase other currently seemingly more prosperous markets could be a dangerous decision. When small-cap US stocks start to lead the rally, historically it often marks the last hurrah before liquidity dries up at the end of a bull market. The Russell 2000 index has risen more than 45% since its 2025 low, but most of its constituent stocks have relatively poor profitability and are highly sensitive to interest rate changes. If the Federal Reserve's monetary policy falls short of expectations, these companies' vulnerabilities will be immediately exposed. Secondly, the AI ​​sector's frenzy exhibits typical bubble characteristics. Both Deutsche Bank's survey and Bridgewater Associates founder Ray Dalio's warnings list the AI ​​bubble as the biggest risk to the market in 2026. The valuations of star companies like Nvidia and Palantir have reached historical highs, and whether their profit growth can support such high valuations is increasingly being questioned. A deeper risk lies in the fact that AI's massive energy consumption may trigger a new round of inflationary pressures, forcing central banks to tighten monetary policy and burst the asset bubble. According to a Bank of America fund manager survey in January, global investor optimism is at its highest level since July 2021, with global growth expectations soaring. Cash holdings have fallen to a record low of 3.2%, and protective measures against market corrections are at their lowest level since January 2018. On one hand, sovereign assets are experiencing a frenzied surge, and investor sentiment is generally optimistic; on the other hand, geopolitical conflicts are escalating. Against this backdrop, Bitcoin's "stagnation" is not simply a matter of "underperforming the market." It's more like a sobering signal, an early warning of greater future risks, and a build-up of momentum for a grander narrative shift. For true long-term investors, this is precisely the time to test their beliefs, resist temptations, and prepare for the coming crises and opportunities.

data.title: 11 Major Application Scenarios of Artificial Intelligence and Cryptocurrency Integration
data.description:
Author: a16z crypto Source: X, @a16zcrypto Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The internet economic landscape has quietly changed. As the open internet is gradually replaced by command input boxes, we can't help but ask: Will artificial intelligence give rise to a more open internet, or will it build new barriers to payment? And who will control all of this—centralized giants or the vast user community?

This is precisely where encryption technology comes in. We have previously discussed the intersection of artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency. In short, blockchain provides a new paradigm for internet service architecture and network construction, creating a decentralized, trustworthy, neutral, and user-controlled network. By reconstructing the underlying economic logic supporting the current internet system, it counteracts the increasingly prominent centralized trend in artificial intelligence systems, helping to build a more open and robust internet.

The idea that encryption technology empowers better AI systems, and vice versa, is not new, but it has long lacked a clear definition. Some convergence scenarios—for example, how to verify "human proof" in the context of the proliferation of low-cost AI systems—have already attracted a large number of developers and users. However, other application scenarios, even if not far off, will likely take several years to materialize. Therefore, this article outlines 11 major application scenarios at the intersection of AI and cryptocurrency, aiming to stimulate discussion on their feasibility and key challenges that need to be overcome. These scenarios are all based on currently developing technologies, covering multiple dimensions from processing massive micro-payments to ensuring human ownership of future AI interactions. 1. Persistent Data and Contextual Information in AI Interactions Author: Scott Duke Kominers (@skominers) The development of generative AI relies heavily on data support, but in many application scenarios, the importance of contextual information—that is, the state and background information related to the interaction—is no less than, if not greater than, that of data. Ideally, whether it's an intelligent agent, a large language model interface, or other applications, AI systems should remember many details such as the type of project the user is working on, their communication style, and preferred programming languages. However, in practice, users often need to repeatedly build context during different interactions within the same application—for example, every time a new version of ChatGPT or a Claude session is launched, not to mention when switching between different systems. Currently, the contextual information of a generative AI application is almost impossible to transfer and reuse in other applications. Leveraging blockchain technology, AI systems can transform key contextual elements into persistent digital assets, loaded at session initiation, and seamlessly transferred between different AI platforms. Furthermore, considering forward compatibility and interoperability are core characteristics of blockchain protocols, blockchain may be the only solution to this problem. A natural application scenario for this technology is in AI-mediated gaming and media—user preferences (from game difficulty to key binding settings) can take effect synchronously across different games and environments. However, its true value lies in knowledge-based applications and specialized AI scenarios (such as coding): the former requires AI to understand the user's knowledge base and learning methods, while the latter demands high operational consistency. Admittedly, some companies have developed customized bots equipped with specific global contexts, but such contexts are usually not portable and cannot interoperate even between different AI systems within an enterprise. Enterprises are only now realizing this problem; the closest existing solution to a general-purpose solution is a customized bot with a fixed, persistent context. However, the portability of context among users within a platform is already beginning to emerge in off-chain scenarios—for example, on the PoE platform, users can rent out their customized bots to others. If such applications are migrated on-chain, the AI ​​systems we interact with daily will be able to share a context layer, integrating key elements of all user digital behaviors. They will be able to instantly understand user preferences, thereby optimizing the interactive experience. Conversely, similar to on-chain intellectual property registration mechanisms, allowing AI to access persistent on-chain contexts has the potential to foster entirely new market interaction models centered around instruction and information modules—for example, users can directly authorize others to use their expertise and monetize it while retaining ownership of their data. Of course, shared context will also unlock many possibilities that are currently unimaginable. 2. A Universal Identity System for Intelligent Agents Author: Sam Broner (@SamBroner) Identity, as an authoritative record defining the attributes of things, is the underlying cornerstone supporting today's digital search, aggregation, and payment systems. Because platforms hide this underlying architecture behind the scenes, the identity perceived by users is only a part of the final product: Amazon assigns unique codes (such as ASINs or FNSKUs) to products, centrally displays product information, and assists users in completing the discovery and purchase process; Facebook's logic is similar—user identity is the core basis for its content push and in-platform content discovery (including Facebook Marketplace product listings, native posts, and paid ads). With the development of artificial intelligence agent technology, this landscape is destined to be rewritten. As more and more enterprises apply intelligent agents to scenarios such as customer service, logistics management, and payment processing, their platform form will no longer be limited to a single-interface application, but will operate across multiple terminals and platforms, accumulating deep contextual information and handling more tasks for users. However, if the identity of an intelligent agent is only bound to a certain market, it will be unable to function effectively in other key scenarios such as email and Slack channels. Therefore, intelligent agents need a unified and portable "digital passport." Without this identity system, we will be unable to determine the payment method of the intelligent agent, verify its version information, query its functional boundaries, clarify its service entity, or trace its reputation record across applications and platforms. The identity system of intelligent agents needs to have multiple functions, including a wallet, an application programming interface (API) registry, update logs, and reputation credentials, to ensure that any interactive interface, such as email, Slack, and other intelligent agents, can interface with it using a unified standard. Without a universal, fundamental component like "identity," each system integration requires building the underlying architecture from scratch, leaving information discovery fragmented and contextual information lost whenever users switch channels or platforms. We are now presented with an opportunity to design the infrastructure for intelligent agents from the ground up. So, how do we build a trusted, neutral identity layer richer than Domain Name System (DNS) records? The answer lies in abandoning the integrated platform model of "identity + discovery + aggregation + payment," allowing intelligent agents to autonomously receive payments, disclose information, operate across multiple ecosystems, and not be locked to a single platform. This is precisely the value of the fusion of artificial intelligence and encryption technology—blockchain networks provide permissionless composability, helping developers create more practical intelligent agents and a better user experience. Currently, vertically integrated solutions from companies like Facebook and Amazon do indeed offer a superior user experience—the core challenge in creating excellent products lies in achieving top-down module collaboration. However, this convenience comes at a high cost, especially given the continuously decreasing software development costs for intelligent agent aggregation, marketing, monetization, and distribution, and the expanding application scenarios. Achieving the user experience level of a vertically integrated platform still requires significant investment. However, building a trusted and neutral identity layer for intelligent agents will allow entrepreneurs to possess their own "digital passport" and stimulate innovation in the distribution and design fields.

3. A Human Authentication Mechanism with Forward Compatibility

Authors: Jay Drain Jr. (@jay_drainjr), Scott Duke Kominers (@skominers)

With the popularization of artificial intelligence, various online interaction scenarios are filled with intelligent robots and agent programs. Deepfake technology and social media manipulation are also emerging one after another, making it increasingly difficult for us to determine whether online interaction objects are real humans.

This crisis of trust is not a future threat, but a reality already in place: from the bot comments on social media platforms to the bot accounts on dating apps, the boundary between reality and virtuality is blurring. In this environment, human identity verification mechanisms have become an indispensable infrastructure. One way to prove human identity is through digital identity credentials (including centralized credentials used by the U.S. Transportation Security Administration (TSA)). Digital identity credentials encompass various information that users can use to verify their identity, including usernames, personal identification numbers, passwords, third-party verifications (such as citizenship, creditworthiness), and other credentials. Decentralization is invaluable here: if this data is stored in a centralized system, the issuer may restrict user access, charge fees, or even assist in surveillance activities. The decentralized model completely reverses this situation—users, rather than platform administrators, control identity information, making identity credentials more secure and resistant to censorship. Unlike traditional identity systems, decentralized human identity verification mechanisms (such as Worldcoin's Proof of Human) allow users to control and safeguard their identity information, and complete human-machine verification in a privacy-preserving, trustworthy, and neutral manner. Just as driver's licenses are not limited by issuance time or location and are valid globally, decentralized human identity verification mechanisms can serve as underlying infrastructure, adaptable to any platform—including platforms yet to be established. In other words, blockchain-based human identity verification mechanisms have backward compatibility for the following reasons: Portability: The relevant protocols are open standards, which can be integrated by any platform. Decentralized human identity verification mechanisms rely on public infrastructure and are under user control, therefore they are fully portable and compatible with all current and future platforms.

  • Permission-free accessibility:Each platform can decide independently whether to recognize this human identity verification identifier, without going through potentially discriminatory intermediary application programming interfaces.

    • The core challenge in this field is implementation and promotion: Although there are currently no large-scale human identity verification application cases, we expect that its adoption rate will accelerate significantly once the number of users reaches a critical mass, several early partnerships are established, and blockbuster applications emerge. Each time an application adopts a digital identity standard, the value of that identity identifier to users increases, attracting more users to apply, which in turn prompts more applications to integrate the identifier to complete human-machine verification.

    Permission-free accessibility:

    7. Micropayment Systems Supporting Revenue Sharing

    Author: Liz Harkavy (@liz_harkavy)

    AI agents and tools such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Copilot provide convenient new ways for people to explore the digital world. But regardless of their advantages and disadvantages, they are also impacting the existing economic model of the open internet. This impact is already beginning to emerge: for example, as more students use AI tools, traffic to educational platforms has declined significantly; several US newspapers have sued OpenAI for copyright infringement. If incentive mechanisms are not adjusted in time, the internet may become increasingly closed, with numerous paywalls and a sharp decline in the number of content creators.

    Of course, we can solve this problem through policy measures, but before relevant legislation takes effect, a series of technical solutions have emerged. The most promising (and technically most complex) solution is to embed a revenue-sharing mechanism into the underlying internet architecture. When an AI-driven action facilitates a transaction, all content sources that provided information supporting that decision should receive a share. Affiliate marketing ecosystems have already implemented attribution tracking and revenue sharing, and more advanced technologies can automatically track the information chain and reward all contributors—blockchain technology can clearly play a key role in traceability. However, building such a system requires supporting new infrastructure, especially micropayment systems with the following characteristics: the ability to handle small transactions across multiple sources, attribution protocols that fairly measure the value of various contributions, and governance models that ensure transparency and fairness. Numerous existing blockchain tools, such as rollup (blockchain scaling solutions) and Layer 2 (second-layer networks), AI-native financial institution Catena Labs, and the financial infrastructure protocol 0xSplits, have demonstrated application potential, enabling near-zero-cost transactions and more granular revenue splitting. Blockchain technology can construct complex smart agent payment systems through various mechanisms: Micro-payment splitting: Micro-payments can be distributed to multiple data providers. A single user interaction can automatically pay corresponding rewards to all information contributors through smart contracts. Executable traceable payments: Smart contracts support triggering traceable payments after a transaction is completed, compensating the information sources that facilitated the transaction under the premise of full transparency and traceability. Complex Programmable Payment Splitting: Achieving highly flexible programmable revenue distribution by enforcing distribution rules through code, rather than relying on centralized decision-making, and establishing trustless financial relationships between autonomous intelligent agents. As these emerging technologies mature, they are expected to create a completely new economic model for the media industry, covering the entire value creation chain from creators and platforms to users. 8. Blockchain as an Intellectual Property and Traceability Registration System Author: Scott Duke Kominers (@skominers) The rise of generative artificial intelligence has made efficient and programmable intellectual property registration and tracking mechanisms a necessity—both for clarifying intellectual property ownership and for building business models around intellectual property access, sharing, and secondary creation. The existing intellectual property framework relies on high-cost intermediaries and adopts a post-event rights protection model, which is no longer suitable for the needs of the artificial intelligence era—an era in which AI can instantly digest massive amounts of content and generate derivative works with a single click. We need an open public registration system to provide clear ownership certificates for intellectual property, facilitating efficient connections for creators, while also supporting direct access by AI and other internet applications. Blockchain technology is an ideal choice for achieving this goal: it allows creators to complete intellectual property registration without intermediaries, providing tamper-proof traceability; at the same time, third-party applications can easily identify, authorize, and use these intellectual property rights. Some are skeptical about whether technology can protect intellectual property, which is understandable—after all, the first two stages of internet development, and the current AI revolution, seem to have weakened the protection of intellectual property. One key issue is that most current business models based on intellectual property focus on restricting the creation of derivative works, rather than incentivizing derivative creation and monetization. However, programmable intellectual property infrastructure not only helps creators, franchisors, and brands clarify intellectual property ownership in the digital space, but also fosters business models centered on shared intellectual property, empowering generative artificial intelligence and other digital applications. In fact, this transforms the main threat of generative artificial intelligence to the creative industries into a new development opportunity. Even during the previous NFT boom, creators began experimenting with new models: many companies, based on NFT assets on Ethereum and relying on the CC0 (Creative Commons Zero-License) brand building model, built network effects and achieved value accumulation. Recently, more and more infrastructure providers have begun developing related protocols, and even creating dedicated blockchains (such as Story Protocol), to provide standardized, composable registration and licensing services for intellectual property. Some artists have already licensed their artistic styles and works for secondary creation through protocols such as Alias, Neura, and Titles. Furthermore, Incention's Emergence licensing project invites fans to co-create science fiction universes and character designs, and builds a blockchain registration system based on Story Protocol to record creator information for various content. 9. Web Crawler Technology that Helps Content Creators Earn Revenue Author: Carra Wu (@carrawu) Currently, the AI ​​agent with the highest product-market fit is not an intelligent program in the coding or entertainment fields, but a web crawler—it can autonomously browse the internet, collect data, and decide which links to crawl. It is estimated that nearly half of internet traffic currently comes from non-human entities. Web crawlers often ignore the rules in the robots.txt file—a file intended to inform automated crawlers whether to access target websites, but lacking enforcement power in practice—and the data they collect becomes a core resource for many global tech giants to build competitive barriers. Worse still, website operators have to pay for these uninvited "visitors," consuming bandwidth and computing resources to deal with the constant stream of anonymous crawlers. To address this issue, content delivery network (CDN) providers like Cloudflare have launched web crawler blocking services. However, these solutions are ultimately stopgap measures and should not be considered essential. We previously pointed out that the original contract of the internet—the economic agreement between content creators and platform distributors—may be crumbling. Relevant data confirms this trend: in the past twelve months, an increasing number of website operators have begun blocking AI web crawlers. In July 2024, only about 9% of the world's top 10,000 websites prohibited AI web crawlers; this proportion has now climbed to 37%. As website operators continue to upgrade their technical capabilities and user dissatisfaction grows, this proportion will continue to rise. So, besides paying content delivery networks to block suspected web crawler requests, can we find a more moderate solution? Rather than allowing AI web crawlers to occupy network resources accessible to human users for free, perhaps they should pay for the right to collect data. This is precisely where blockchain technology can begin: in this scenario, each web crawler agent will hold a certain amount of encrypted assets and negotiate on-chain with the target website's "gatekeeper" agent or paywall protocol via the x402 protocol. (Of course, this solution faces a challenge: since the 1990s, the robots.txt protocol (i.e., the robot exclusion standard) has been deeply embedded in the business processes of internet companies. Driving change requires large-scale industry collaboration or the participation of mainstream content delivery networks such as Cloudflare.) Meanwhile, human users can complete human-machine verification through World ID (see above) and access website content for free. In this model, content creators and website operators receive corresponding compensation when their data is collected; while human users can continue to enjoy the free flow of information on the internet. 10. Personalized and Privacy-Friendly Advertising That Doesn't Infringe on Privacy Author: Matt Gleason (@mg_486662) Artificial intelligence has begun to change the way we shop online, but what if the ads we see every day could become truly useful? The reasons people dislike ads are obvious: irrelevant ads are pure visual noise. At the same time, not all personalized ads are offensive—it's highly customized AI ads pushed based on massive amounts of user data that feel like a privacy violation. Furthermore, some apps monetize by forcing users to watch ads through paywalls (such as streaming content or game levels). Encryption technology can help solve these problems, offering new ideas for restructuring the advertising industry. Combining blockchain with personalized AI agents can find a balance between the "irrelevance" and "privacy violation" of advertising, pushing ads based on user-defined preferences. More importantly, this model does not require the public disclosure of user data and can directly pay users who share data or participate in advertising interactions. Achieving this goal requires meeting the following technical conditions: Low-fee digital payments: To pay users who participate in advertising interactions (views, clicks, conversions), businesses need to complete high-frequency, small-amount payments. For large-scale implementation, a high-speed, high-throughput payment system with extremely low fees is essential. Privacy-protected data verification: AI agents need to be able to prove that target users conform to specific demographic characteristics, and zero-knowledge proof technology can complete this verification process while protecting user privacy. Incentive Model Design: If the internet fully adopts a micropayment monetization model (such as the previously mentioned reward of less than $0.05 per interaction), users can choose to watch ads to receive small rewards, thus transforming the current "data extraction" model into a "user engagement" model. For decades, online and offline advertising practitioners have been striving to achieve precision marketing. Re-examining the advertising industry from the perspective of the integration of encryption technology and artificial intelligence holds promise for truly realizing the practical value of advertising—personalized yet privacy-preserving, and achieving a win-win situation for all parties: for developers and advertisers, this unlocks a more sustainable and user-centric incentive model; for users, it provides more effective ways to explore the digital world. This model will not only not reduce the value of ad placements, but will actually enhance their commercial potential. It also holds the promise of breaking down the current deeply entrenched predatory advertising economy and building a more human-centered new ecosystem—an ecosystem where users are no longer exploited "products" but equal participants. 11. AI Companions Owned and Controlled by Humans Author: Guy Wuollet (@guywuolletjr) Today, many people spend more time on electronic devices than offline social interactions, and a significant portion of that time is spent interacting with AI models and AI-generated content. These models have already begun providing companionship services, appearing in entertainment, information acquisition, interest cultivation, and children's education. It's easy to imagine that in the near future, AI companions used for education, healthcare, legal advice, and emotional support will become the mainstream interaction objects for humans. Future AI companions will possess unlimited patience and be able to customize according to individual user needs. Their role will transcend that of tools or robotic servants, becoming cherished emotional connections. Therefore, the question of who will own and control this relationship—the user, or corporations and other intermediaries—becomes crucial. If you have concerns about the content moderation and censorship mechanisms of social media over the past decade, this issue will become even more complex and personal in the future. One not-so-new viewpoint (see related discussions here and here) is that censorship-resistant hosting platforms like blockchain are the most viable path to building uncensorable, user-controlled AI systems. Admittedly, individuals can run local models or purchase their own graphics processors, but most people either cannot afford the cost or lack the necessary technical knowledge. While the widespread adoption of AI companions will take time, the related technologies are developing rapidly: text-based AI companions have achieved a high degree of human-likeness; the realism of visual virtual avatars has also greatly improved; and blockchain performance continues to optimize. Further improvements to the user experience of encrypted applications are needed to make uncensorable AI companions more usable. Fortunately, wallets like Phantom have significantly simplified blockchain interaction processes; the emergence of embedded wallets, key authentication, and account abstraction technologies allows users to manage their wallets independently without remembering complex mnemonic phrases. Furthermore, high-throughput trusted computing technology based on optimistic consensus and zero-knowledge proof coprocessors will also help humans build meaningful and lasting relationships with their digital partners. In the near future, the focus of discussion will no longer be "when will highly realistic digital partners and virtual avatars appear," but rather "who, and what kind of entities, will have the right to control them."

data.title: Europe Still Can’t Decide If Polymarket Is Finance or Gambling — So It Keeps Banning It
data.description:
A Patchwork of Bans Across Europe

Hungary and Portugal block the crypto prediction market as regulators across Europe remain split on whether Polymarket belongs under financial rules or gambling law.

Hungary and Portugal have moved to restrict access to crypto-based prediction market Polymarket, deepening regulatory pressure across Europe and underscoring a growing identity crisis over how such platforms should be classified — as financial markets or illegal gambling.

lol @PM_ViktorOrban banned polymarket xd

right wing bros only hear the good things about him coz trump praises him so he can have a pawn in the EU

remember my post how crypto is banned in hungary thats why i withdrew everything on the 27th of december?

guess that was good pic.twitter.com/ekutBieL89

— Deethz (@NullFUD) January 19, 2026

In Hungary, the Szabályozott Tevékenységek Felügyeleti Hatósága (SZTFH) temporarily blocked access to Polymarket’s domain and subdomains, citing the “forbidden organization of gambling activities.” According to an official notice released Friday, the block will remain in effect while the regulator completes its review.

Local users attempting to access the platform from Hungarian IP addresses reported seeing a warning message issued directly by the authority, confirming the restriction was live.

In Portugal, the Gaming Regulation and Inspection Service (SRIJ) ordered Polymarket to wind down operations, although the platform remained accessible to users as of Monday, according to local outlet Rádio Renascença, suggesting enforcement was still in progress.

Portuguese regulators reportedly deemed Polymarket’s activity illegal on two fronts: the platform lacks the required gambling license, and political betting is banned nationwide. The watchdog also flagged roughly 4 million euros ($4.6 million) in wagers placed on Portuguese presidential races shortly before results were announced — raising concerns about potential insider trading and information asymmetry.

Regulators are taking aim at crypto prediction markets and Portugal just stepped into the spotlight.

Portuguese authorities have ordered the crypto-based prediction platform Polymarket to cease operations within 48 hours, citing national law that bans betting on political events… pic.twitter.com/ay94YA2xfI

— AndreWGMI (@AndreWGMI) January 20, 2026

The latest actions come just one week after Ukraine blocked Polymarket, classifying its services as unlicensed gambling under national law.

Hungary and Portugal now join a growing list of jurisdictions that have restricted or blocked the platform over similar concerns, including France, Belgium, Poland, Switzerland and Singapore.

BREAKING: France moves to ban crypto betting platform Polymarket after a French trader made $48M on Trump victory, calling it “illegal gambling.” pic.twitter.com/YfuK8Vnzxt

— Viktor 🧡 (@s0meone_u_know) November 7, 2024

France’s National Gaming Authority said in November 2024 it planned to block Polymarket for failing to comply with domestic gambling regulations. Switzerland followed later that month, when its Gambling Supervisory Authority classified the platform as unlicensed gambling and ordered access restrictions.

Poland added Polymarket to its registry of prohibited gambling websites on Jan. 8, 2025, while Singapore blocked access days later as part of a broader crackdown on unlicensed platforms. Belgium’s gambling regulator took similar action on Jan. 30, 2025, citing violations of national gambling legislation.

According to Polymarket’s website, the platform is already geoblocked in 33 countries, highlighting how fragmented enforcement has become.

Markets or Betting Slips? Regulators Remain Divided

At the center of the crackdown lies a fundamental disagreement over what Polymarket actually is.

The platform allows users to trade contracts tied to real-world outcomes, with prices determined by market participants rather than a bookmaker. Supporters argue this structure mirrors financial markets, where prices reflect collective expectations and information.

1/ Nevada has filed a civil lawsuit to block Polymarket from offering sports event contracts in the state.

This is the first time a U.S. regulator has taken Polymarket itself to court.

2/ Nevada is arguing that Polymarket’s sports contracts are illegal gambling under state law… pic.twitter.com/UucVebz4Qh

— Astera (@asteraappai) January 19, 2026

Regulators across Europe have taken a sharply different view, repeatedly classifying prediction markets as gambling — particularly when contracts involve elections or geopolitical events. This regulatory gray zone has left Polymarket operating in a legal no-man’s-land, where it can resemble a derivatives exchange to traders and a betting shop to authorities.

Hungary’s move also follows heightened scrutiny of Polymarket after a controversial wager linked to geopolitical events.

On Jan. 3, a Polymarket account placed a large bet predicting the removal of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro — just hours before U.S. forces captured him in a military operation. The trade reportedly netted around $400,000 in profit, triggering concerns about insider knowledge and misuse of political information.

The incident prompted U.S. lawmakers to propose legislation aimed at restricting political prediction market trading by government officials, further intensifying global scrutiny of the sector.

Despite mounting regulatory pressure, demand for prediction markets continues to surge. Trading volume hit a record $701.7 million on Jan. 12, with competitor Kalshi accounting for roughly two-thirds of activity.

Polymarket did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the latest European enforcement actions.

data.title: Why is Polymarket's late-day trading strategy more likely to lead to losses?
data.description:
Author: Claire, Hubble Research; Source: X, @MeetHubble

In the current Polymarket trading ecosystem, the "Tail Market Strategy" is becoming a popular strategy.

Its logic is simple and enticing: specifically targeting markets that have entered the "Proposed" stage and whose prices are stuck at 0.99. Traders believe that at this point, the event is over, the results are public, and by buying and holding and waiting only 2 hours for settlement, they can reliably earn a 1% risk-free spread. Under the ideal assumption of high turnover, this seems like a "holy grail" with an astonishing annualized return.

However, on-chain data reveals a different picture: Many active addresses focusing on this strategy not only failed to see long-term net worth growth but also experienced significant drawdowns. Why can't a 99% win rate generate positive returns? The Hubble research team believes the root cause lies in a systematically underestimated variable: the time cost of the dispute mechanism. We audited the full-cycle data of 943 dispute cases in Polymarket's history, and the results show that under certain high-risk market structures, once the time value of frozen funds is factored in, the theoretically positive expected value (Positive EV) quickly turns into a negative value. I. Root Cause of the Mechanism: Disputes Are Not Accidents, But Endogenous Game Themselves To understand the source of the losses, we must first understand Polymarket's settlement layer—the UMA Optimistic Oracle. Many people view disputes as random "black swan" events, but under UMA's mechanism design, disputes are actually a **economically incentivized game**. According to the rules, if a challenger successfully overturns a proposed result, they can take 50% of the proposer's deposit (usually $375 or higher). This means that disputes do not entirely depend on what the "truth" is, but rather on whether "the rules have room for interpretation". When a market at 0.99 has ambiguity or a flawed chain of evidence, initiating a dispute becomes a call option with a positive expected value. For traders entering at 0.99, you think you're earning interest, but in reality, you're selling insurance with complex legal risks without any risk premium. II. Black Swan Retrospective: How Does Liquidity Dry Up? We will use two famous "black swan" events to reconstruct how this risk can destroy accounts at the micro level.

1. The Zelenskyy Suit Case

  • Subject: Will Zelenskyy wear a suit before July?

  • Trading Volume: $242 million

  • Background: Zelenskyy appeared in a video wearing a dark collared shirt. Although the public generally considered this not to be a suit, the rules did not provide an exclusive definition of the form of a "suit".

  • Consequence: In order to compete for margin, the players used this semantic ambiguity to launch five consecutive rounds of technical challenges.

  • Reaction: The market status fluctuated repeatedly between Proposed and Disputed, and funds were forcibly locked up for 8.5 days. For funds that entered at 0.99, this meant a complete loss of liquidity for up to a week. In a bull market environment, this opportunity cost erosion far exceeds the 1% nominal return.

2. The Stranger Things Case

  • Target: Will Eleven die in Stranger Things?

  • Background: Before the series aired, highly credible spoilers leaked online, and the price quickly rose to 0.99. Consequences: Although the spoiler was ultimately proven accurate, opponents launched a controversy on the grounds that "unofficial sources are unverifiable." Reactions: Funds were frozen until the series was officially released and official evidence was produced. This reveals a core risk in the entertainment market: a time lag between **truth** and **verifiable evidence**. Buying at 0.99 essentially involves bearing the uncertainty of this time lag. III. Avoidance Guide: Stay Away from Markets "Defined by Words" We conducted a penetrating analysis of 943 historical disputed data points and found that Polymarket's risk distribution exhibits **extreme structural discontinuities**. The safety of a 0.99 betting line is determined not by its popularity, but by its resolution source. **Politics:** Dispute rate as high as 17.7%. Due to the large number of subjective terms (such as "Likely", "Officially") and information asymmetry, this section is a high-risk area for disputes. **Entertainment:** Dispute rate 12.5%. The main risk stems from the verifiability of evidence. **Sports:** Dispute rate < 2%. Based on objective scores, the risk is extremely low. This illustrates that applying an indiscriminate 0.99 strategy to the political or entertainment sectors is tantamount to walking on a minefield. IV. Recalculating: How Does Time Cost Eat Up Profits? If time cost is taken into account, what is the expected value of this strategy? We constructed an EV model that includes **opportunity cost of capital**: Assuming a purchase price of 0.99 (expected return of 1%), the probability of political market disputes is 17.7%, the average capital lock-up loss is 7% (based on an average lock-up period of 3 days and bull market capital efficiency), and the probability of losing and returning to zero is 5.3%. The calculation formula is: EV = (Probability of successful redemption x 1%) + (Probability of winning a dispute x (1% - 7%)) + (Probability of losing a dispute x -100%). After substituting the data, we get: The true EV of the 0.99 strategy (Politics type) ≈ -5.22% This is the mathematical explanation for the loss: Participating in the late-day trading of high-risk sectors without proper screening results in a net loss for every order placed mathematically. V. Conclusion In summary, the key to the success or failure of the late-day trading strategy lies not in timing, but in target selection. True alpha comes from avoiding those "toxic assets" with negative mathematical expectation. **Sector Circuit Breaker:** Automatically blocks late-day signals from highly subjective sectors such as Politics and Entertainment. **Semantic Risk Control:** Utilizes NLP technology to identify high-risk ambiguous words in rules, proactively avoiding "literal wordplay." **EV Dynamic Calculation:** Calculates implied odds in real-time, ensuring that the expected return on every trade covers potential time costs.
